coredata.dc:description: Simazine, a widely used herbicide, poses significant environmental and health risks due to its persistence in soil and water. This study investigates high photocatalytic degradation of simazine using 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites in combination with gold tri-octahedron (AuTOH) nanoparticles as the composite nanocatalyst. The materials were synthesized via a facile method, leveraging unique properties of 2D niobium carbide (Nb2CTx) and UiO-66 metal-organic frameworks functionalized with reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs). The integration of Au nanoparticles could harness localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects, thereby increasing light absorption, generating more reactive species, and improving photocatalytic activity. The 3D-printed structures address the aggregation issues typical of powder-form photocatalysts, enhancing their stability and recyclability. Degradation efficiency of simazine under visible and UV irradiation was evaluated by measuring both UV-Vis adsorption and GC-MS. The integration of AuTOH nanoparticles with 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites demonstrated superior photocatalytic performance compared to pristine Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs. This research highlights the potential of integrating plasmonic nanoparticles with 3D-printed nanomaterials for environmental remediations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of synthesis process of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites.

(a) Schematics of AuTOH NP immobilization onto 3D printed scaffold surfaces through APTES treatment, (b) FTIR spectra of surface chemical modified samples, (c) 3D printed scaffold with nonfunctionalized (i) and NH2-functionalized (ii) after immersion in AuTOH solution, and (d-g) the EDS mapping of 3D printed with AuTOH. The insert scale is 100 μm.

FE-SEM images of (a) Nb2CTx, (b) UiO-66@rGQDs, (c) Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, and (d) 3D printed scaffold of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites. (e) AuTOH NPs which were attached to the surface of the 3D printed scaffold. (i-vii) EDS mapping images of 3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs with AuTOHs onto the surface (elemental of Nb, C, Zr, O, F, Cl, and Au).

(a) XRD spectra of Nb2AlC, Nb2CTx, UiO-66, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs. (b) Raman spectra of Nb2CTx, UiO-66, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs. (c) XPS spectra of Nb2AlC, Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (d) Nb 3d, (e) Zr 3d, and (f) C 1s scan for Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs.

Optical data of Nb2CTx, UiO-66, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites: (a) confocal fluorescence images, (b) UV–Vis DRS spectra, (c) PL spectra excited at 365 nm; photocurrent response transient of (d) Nb2CTx, UiO-66, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs and (e) 3D composite samples with and without AuTOH.

(a) Simazine decomposition efficiency and (b) first-order pseudo kinetics of Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, and AuTOH-3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs under UV irradiation. (c) Simazine degradation and (d) first-order pseudo kinetics of 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, and AuTOH-3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs under visible light. (e) Simazine photodegradation efficiencies of AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs according to pH factor (pH=3, 6, 9 and 12). (f) The stability of AuTOH-3D-composites for simazine degradation for nine-times recyclability.
Putative degradation and mineralization pathways of simazine in the AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs photocatalyts system.

(a) The energy band-gap spectra and (b) Mott-Schottky diagram of of UiO-66@rGQDs. (c) The simazine photodegradation mechanism for AuTOH-3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs system. (d) ESR spectra of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − using AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposite photocatalyst.
Rate constants and simazine degradation efficiency by the catalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simazine, a widely used herbicide, poses significant environmental and health risks due to its persistence in soil and water. This study investigates high photocatalytic degradation of simazine using 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites in combination with gold tri-octahedron (AuTOH) nanoparticles as the composite nanocatalyst. The materials were synthesized via a facile method, leveraging unique properties of 2D niobium carbide (Nb2CTx) and UiO-66 metal-organic frameworks functionalized with reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs). The integration of Au nanoparticles could harness localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects, thereby increasing light absorption, generating more reactive species, and improving photocatalytic activity. The 3D-printed structures address the aggregation issues typical of powder-form photocatalysts, enhancing their stability and recyclability. Degradation efficiency of simazine under visible and UV irradiation was evaluated by measuring both UV-Vis adsorption and GC-MS. The integration of AuTOH nanoparticles with 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites demonstrated superior photocatalytic performance compared to pristine Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs. This research highlights the potential of integrating plasmonic nanoparticles with 3D-printed nanomaterials for environmental remediations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The octahedral UiO-66@rGQDs particles dispersed into the pores and on the surface of Nb2CTx forming a strong interfacial interaction between them. Through the self-assembly method, AuTOH NPs (115 nm diameter) were successfully attached to the 3D printed scaffold-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs exhibiting outstanding photocatalytic activity. Showing a remarkable rate constant of 0.19073 min−1 within 12 min under UV irradiation and 0.02315 min⁻¹ within 80 min under visible light, AuTOHs-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs catalyst showed significantly increased activity compared to other catalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Simazine, a member of chloro-s-triazine herbicide family, has been used for agricultural weed control strategies since its utilization from the late 1950s [1]. Its effectiveness to combat broad-leaved weeds, annual grasses and pre-emergence action of disrupting the root growth in germinating seedlings have been indispensable to the farmers [2]. However, its prolonged use and extensive applications have raised significant environmental and health concerns. Exposure to simazine presents various health risks with inhalation and ingestion via the dermal contact [3]. Acute and chronic toxicity manifestations range from skin irritations and organ damage to genetic mutations and disturbances in crop production [4] along with adverse effects on reproductive systems [5]. The persistence of simazine in soil and its ability to leach into groundwater has contaminated water sources, posing risks to both human and aquatic life. The complex sorption behavior of simazine in soil, influenced by soil composition, organic content, and moisture levels complicates its management and remediation [6]. While the practice of breaking up and turning over the soil can decrease the amount of simazine that washes off into nearby water sources, they are not always sufficient to prevent environmental contamination. Additionally, degradation of simazine by the natural microbial activity is often slow and inadequate to address contamination concerns [2].

In light of these challenges, innovative approaches to simazine remediation are increasingly explored by phytoremediation [7], photocatalytic mineralization [8], and synergistic oxidation [9]. Among these, photocatalytic techniques are promising as sustainable and effective approaches to remove simazine from contaminated environments. By employing light-activated catalysts, simazine molecules can be degraded into non-toxic byproducts. This approach can be a potential solution to persistent issue of simazine contamination, providing a pathway towards mitigating its environmental impact to safeguard human health.

In the past decade, strong and highly efficient photocatalysts based on 2D materials have emerged as promising platforms including MXene-based nanocomposites [10], Mo single atom dispersed g-C3N4 [11], and nitrogen-doped carbon skeleton [12]. The 2D niobium carbide (Nb2CTx) MXene materials, renowned for its exceptional electrical conductivity and 2D nanosheet structure are excellent catalysts due to their unique properties. Their lower Fermi level compared to other MXenes such as Ti3C2Tx enhances movements of the carriers to the photocatalyst surfaces, promoting efficient charge separation and utilization [13]. Nb2CTx shows durability, hydrophilicity, and outstanding electrical properties for energy storage [13] and functional memories. [14]. Moreover, 2D film of Nb2CTx can increase the capture of a broad spectrum of light, including ultraviolet-visible radiation, leading to high photocatalytic activity for organics degradation [15]. The abundance of hydrophilic functional groups onto the surface of Nb2CTx (-O, -OH, and -F) enables strong interactions with other photocatalytic materials, further enhancing its performance.

A ZnO@Nb2CTx@carbon nitride nanosheet visible photocatalyst was more efficient than those of either pure ZnO or carbon nitride nanosheet for enrofloxacin degradation [16]. The Nb2CTx/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials were synthesized for the photodegradation of methylene blue, demonstrating 2.96-times higher efficiency compared to pure Bi2WO6 [17]. Nb2CTx/AgNPs-CTAB was found to be useful in energy storage devices [18]. The enhanced photocatalytic performances of these materials can optimize the light harvesting, thereby facilitating photogenerated carrier separation to achieve higher quantum efficiency in photocatalysis.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are effective candidates for environmental remediation, particularly in photocatalysis area. Among the numerous MOFs studied, UiO-66 is the most widely explored material because of its exceptional stability, extensive surface area, tunable pore sizes with pellet [19] and hierarchical structures [20]. Despite its remarkable properties, UiO-66 exhibits certain limitations, hindering its performance in photocatalysis. However, one of its significant drawbacks is its inherent low conductivity, which can impede rapid transfer of charge carriers generated during photocatalytic reactions, leading to increased recombination rates and decreased overall efficiency [21].

To address these challenges and further application of UiO-66 in photocatalysis, an attempt is made to integrate UiO-66 with co-catalysts such as noble metal nanoparticles or semiconductor materials. The reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) are considered as co-catalysts due to their abundant resource availability, high electron conductivity, large specific surface area, excellent optical properties, eminent PL properties, and environmental friendliness [22]. rGQDs are luminescent nanocarbon materials based on graphene, inheriting the unique structural and physicochemical properties of graphene. These materials have wide-ranging applications in various optical fields such as fuel cells, biosensors, bioimaging, environmental monitoring, and catalysis [23]. The hybridization of UiO-66 with rGQDs can aim to address the shortcomings of UiO-66 by harnessing synergistic effects between these materials. Furthermore, the presence of rGQDs within the framework of UiO-66 provides a large surface area and porosity, while at the same time offering good electrical conductivity and mechanical strength properties, enabling interactions with other materials and enhancing photocatalytic performance.

In this research, Nb2CTx and UiO66@rGQDs are combined judicially to construct ternary heterostructured photocatalyst viz., Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites to investigate the degradation of simazine. The unique properties of these nanocomposites, including their high surface area, enhanced light absorption, and synergistic effects between the components make them promising for efficient removal of simazine. In typical photocatalytic reactions, finely dispersed powder-form nano-scale are investigated, realizing that traditional powder-form of photocatalysts are prone to aggregation, resulting in decreased surface area and catalytic activity as well as difficulties in recovery. To address these challenges, 3D-printed structures are proposed in this study, hoping that these structures can offer significant increase in surface area, thereby enhancing light absorption and facilitating mass transport. Additionally, they mitigate aggregation phenomena, improve stability, and enhance recyclability of the samples in photocatalytic processes [24] and antibiotic degradation [25].

Further modification of 3D printed scaffold surface with plasmonic nanoparticles is also used to enhance photocatalytic performance of 3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs composites using AuNP mixtures [26] and nanosheets [27]. AuNPs can act as an electron sink exhibiting the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) in response to incident light [28]. The presence of AuNPs can therefore, broaden absorption spectrum of the nanocomposite to be utilized for a extended light. Also, Au nanoparticles with their high stability and oxidation resistance can protect photocatalytic materials from photocorrosion by providing stable interface [29]. By integrating Au nanoparticles in 3D printed nanocomposites, the material benefits can be achieved from enhanced charge separation, increased light absorption, and improved stability [30].

The structural and photocatalytic properties of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs are studied using various analytical techniques. Photocatalytic performance for simazine degradation is evaluated and compared with powder samples, 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs samples, and 3D-printed composite samples coated with AuTOH nanoparticles. The 3D-printed nanocomposites with AuTOH coating exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity, achieving the removal of 90.79 % and a kinetic reaction constant of 0.1936 min⁻¹ under UV irradiation. Degradation pathways with possible byproducts and intermediates during simazine degradation along with detailed mechanisms for simazine degradation are proposed.

Gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4.3H2O), niobium aluminum carbide MAX phase (Nb2AlC MAX), sodium citrate tribasic dehydrate (Na3Ctr), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), terephthalic acid (H2BDC), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium bromide (NaBr), L-ascorbic acid (AA), and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) were all purchased by Merck (St. Louis USA). N,N-dimethylformamide and acetic acid were purchased from Duksan, (Gwangju, Korea). Anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH), and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were supplied from Daejung (Siheung, Korea). Graphite powder was procured from Alfa Aesar (USA) and hydrochloric acid was purchased from Daejung (Siheung, Republic of Korea). Zirconium (IV) chloride (ZrCl4) and sulfuctants were supplied from Tokyo Chemical Industry (TCI), (Tokyo, Japan). Resin (CUKL89CL, nontoxic and clear was supplied from Carima, Co., Ltd. Korea). Deionized water (DI with a resistivity: 18 MΩ cm, conductivity: 0.055 µS/cm) was used in the experiments.
The synthesis of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposite is presented in Fig. 1.

rGO was synthesized using the modified protocol [25]; the obtained rGO powder was dried at ∼60°C and stored at room temperature for the synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs). Reduced GQDs were synthesized using the top-down approach to fragment graphene sheets into quantum dots following a facile and efficient approach, and rGQDs were generated from 0.5 g of rGO using hyperthermal treatment at 200°C for 5 h in 20 mL of DMF. After cooling the reaction mixture to room temperature, the rGQD samples were filtered using a 0.1-micrometer membrane to remove large black precipitates, allowing rGQDs to pass through filtrate to collect the solid. The rGQDs were kept in DMF for the subsequent synthesis of UiO-66@rGQDs. Throughout the process, safety precautions, including proper ventilation, protective equipment, and handling of chemicals were observed to ensure safe working environment.

To prepare UiO-66@rGQDs, 93.2 mg of ZrCl4, 66.4 mg H2BDC, and 5 mL of the previously synthesized rGQDs were mixed in 45 mL of DMF in a vial without agitation. After the reaction, UiO-66@rGQDs were separated via centrifugation, washed few times with DMF and ethanol, and subsequently dewetted overnight at 60°C under vacuum conditions.

Nb2CTx was prepared by thoroughly mixing 1 g of Nb2AlC with 1 g of LiF/HCl in a Teflon autoclave to ensure homogeneity, the mixture was then subjected to hyperthermal treatment. Prolonged reaction time (16 h) was needed to etch the Al layer of Nb2AlC to form Nb2CTx. The resulting sample was carefully washed several times with a solution of 0.1 M HCl and DI water to adjust the pH at 6 by centrifugation. Then, Nb2CTx multilayer was collected and dried overnight at 60°C under vacuum conditions.

UiO-66@rGQDs and Nb2CTx were combined in a ratio of 3:10 by mass in DI water and stirred for 6 h at room temperature to ensure complete mixing and interaction between the materials. After stirring, centrifugation Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs were separated by centrifugation. The precipitate was collected and vacuum-dried at 60°C to remove any remaining solvent and moisture, yielding the final product of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs in powder form for further characterization and applications.

Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs ink was prepared by mixing Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs powder and clear ink from Carima. These were ground to fine powder using a typical mechanical method. Resin ink was added immediately to disperse the powder mixture. After 2 h of magnetic stirring and 30 min of shaking, they were ultrasonicated for 30 min before being used for printing. With this process, slurry attained exceptionally high homogeneity before being fed into the printer and the nanocomposite mix was obtained according to the design criteria. This mixing step promotes the uniform distribution, mitigating insufficient contact between AuTOHs and Nb2CTₓ/UiO-66@rGQDs. The 3D-printed composite scaffolds with a gyroid structure of the sizes (1×1×1) cm were printed at room temperature via DLP-type (digital light processing) printer. The 3D-printed samples were washed multiple times with ethanol after printing to remove any residual ink, then dried and stored in a vacuum desiccator for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of Au nanoparticles onto surface of amino-capped 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The AuTOH nanoparticles were synthesized via seed-mediated growth approach. The obtained product was preserved in glass vials and stored at 4°C for further utilization. The surface of 3D printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs scaffolds was modified with amino groups for self-deposition of AuTOH on top of it as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. First, the 3D-printed nanocomposites were hydrolyzed by immersing in a 0.5 M NaOH solution for 18 h to create hydroxyl and carboxyl groups onto the surface. Subsequently, amine linkages on the surface of 3D printed samples were formed through immersion in ethanol solution containing 3% APTES for 2 h. FTIR spectra in Fig. 2 b suggest the successful amine modification in 3D-printed scaffold surface. The samples were then rinsed several times by ultrasonication for 10 min in ethanol and subsequently dried at 40°C. The final modified products were immersed in an AuTOH solution for 16 h. The final samples were washed with DI water three times, sealed in a vacuum desiccator and kept overnight to stabilize AuTOH layer onto the surface of 3D printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs samples. The images shown in Fig. 2 c and EDS mapping displayed in Fig. 2 d-g showed successful attachment of AuTOH to the 3D-printed sample.

Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw InVia confocal microscopy, Wotton-under-Edge, UK) was employed to examine the vibrational modes and structural properties. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were conducted to investigate luminescent properties of the samples, which provided information on their optical properties. Confocal fluorescence microscopy was employed to visualize and characterize fluorescence emission of the samples.

Photocurrent response transients and Mott-Schottky spectra were measured using an electrochemical workstation (CHI6005E). The working electrodes were prepared onto fluorine-doped tinoxide glass (FTO) plates with distinct preparation methods for the powder and the 3D-printed samples. For powder samples, 5 mg was thoroughly mixed with Nafion, ethanol, and DI water, then the mixture was placed onto the FTO substrate. The 3D-printed scaffold was directly attached to FTO plate using its ink, taking 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte. The electron spin response (ESR) (EMXmicro, Bruker) was used to investigate the reactive oxygen species (ROS), including the hydroxyl and superoxide radicals during the photocatalytic reaction using spin-trapped reagent 5,5-dimethyl-L-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) was recorded using FLS1000 steady state and lifetime system. The photocatalysis of simazine and its decomposed products were analyzed using Shimadzu GC-2023 system (Kyoto, Japan). Detailed experimental parameters for GC-MS analysis are given in Table S1 . The main m/z at 201, 186, and 44 are assigned for simazine to identify simazine as well as the byproducts based on monitoring the specific target m/z values and analyzing the tandem MS fragmentation patterns. Compounds that were successfully detected and confirmed were designated as "P”.

A 5 mg/L of simazine was chosen to conduct photocatalytic experiments, which was the suitable concentration due to limited solubility of simazine in water. For powdered samples, 5 mg of the catalyst was stirred in 50 mL simazine solution before the photocatalytic reaction for at least 30 min. Photocatalytic degradation of simazine was carried out at room temperature using 365 nm UV light (Lightzen, Korea) and 300 W short arc Xenon lamp (TOPTION, China). A 1 mL of simazine solution was aspirated at certain illumination intervals. For the 3D printed samples, the operations for photocatalytic experiments were simpler. Solutions after the illumination were collected directly without centrifugation, thus minimizing the time and effort during sample processing. A series of degradation cycles were performed to evaluate the reusability and stability of AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs composite. In each cycle, AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs was used to catalyze the degradation of simazine, and the 3D nanocomposite was recovered, thoroughly washed with DI water, ethanol, and then dried. The material obtained was reused for the next degradation cycle to measure degradation efficiency after each cycle to monitor any deterioration in the catalytic activity.

The structure, shape, morphology, distribution, and elemental analysis of Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, AuTOH, 3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs and AuTOH-3D-printed nanocomposites were characterized using FE-SEM and EDS analysis, and depicted in Fig. 3. The formation of Nb2CTx multilayers reveals typical accordion-like structure after etching, indicating successful removal of Al layers from Nb2AlC MAX (see Fig. 3 a and S1). The morphology of UiO-66@rGQDs is illustrated in Fig. 3 b, indicating octahedral shape of UiO-66 remained unaffected by incorporation with rGQDs. The distribution of UiO-66@rGQDs within Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs was also observed. The nano-sized UiO-66@rGQDs particles were dispersed onto the surface and between the layers of Nb2CTx (see Fig. 3 c, and Fig. S2a -d). The metal ions, Zr from UiO-66@rGQDs nanoparticles would interact primarily with the functional groups of Nb2CTx MXene such as -O, -OH, -F, etc. Elemental analysis using EDS was conducted to check the elemental composition of UiO-66@rGQDs and Nb2CTx (Fig. S2d -k). All the elements viz., Nb, C, O, and Zr were observed in EDS results, demonstrating successful formation of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites, which was further confirmed by FE-TEM images (Fig. S2l -m). The Au nanoparticles were characterized by XRD, FE-SEM, and UV-Vis spectra. The AuTOH NPs with a LSPR peak at 580 nm (Fig. S3a), trisoctahedrons shape (Fig. S3b), and nanostructure of fcc gold nanocrystal (Fig. S3c) were observed. FE-SEM images in Fig. 3 d-e, EDS mapping shown in Fig. 3 i-vii and XRD spectra in Fig. S3d reveal uniform distributions of AuTOH particles onto the surface of the 3D printed scaffold, indicating successful coating of Au nanoparticles.

Figure S3e -f shows N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution analysis of UiO-66@rGQDs and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, which suggested that surface area of UiO-66@rGQDs is quite high (2912 m2/g) as demonstrated by high N2 adsorption capacity in the low relative pressure region (P/P0 < 0.2), indicating the presence of mainly microporous pores (less than 2 nm) in UiO-66 structure; when Nb2CTx was added to form the composite, a significant decrease in the surface area of the composite was observed (156 m2/g); this could be because incorporation of Nb2CTx reduced the characteristic microporous pore density of UiO-66, while also playing a role in creating medium and larger pores, leading to a decrease in specific surface area.

Fig. 4 c-f depicts the surface chemistry of photocatalysts and their interactions studies through XPS analysis. The results of Nb2CTx XPS spectrum survey suggests the disappearance of characteristic Al peaks, suggesting that Al has been thoroughly eroded from Nb2AlC block (Fig. 4 c). Elements of UiO-66 and UiO-66@rGQDs were observed through XPS spectra and the contents for the elements C, O, Zr were 63.24%, 31.73%, 5.03% for UiO-66 and 77.4%, 20%, 2.37% for UiO-66@rGQDs, respectively. Notice that carbon content increased after combining UiO-66 with rGQDs, indicating an effective loading of rGQDs onto UiO-66. High-resolution core-level spectra of Nb 3d, C 1s, and Zr 3d elements were identified in the XPS spectrum of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQD nanocomposite mixture, which are consistent with the EDS results.

As illustrated in Fig. 4 d, two pairs of bands observed at 208.81 eV - 205.91 eV and 203.99 eV - 202.92 eV were found in the Nb 3d spectrum, which are designated to O-Nb-O and Nb-C bonds [34]. All the Nb-related peaks in Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs showed higher binding energy values than those of Nb2CTx, which are mainly due to the coverage of UiO-66@rGQDs onto the Nb2CTx surface and there was electron transfer from UiO-66-rGQDs to Nb2CTx. The high-resolution C 1s and Zr 3d XPS spectrum of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs shifted to lower binding energy region compared to UiO-66 sample and to higher energy region compared to Nb2CTx(Fig. 4e-f) [35]. High-resolution C 1s spectra of Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs are shown in Fig. 4 f. The four peaks at 281.12, 283.75, 285.46, and 287.78 eV were deconvoluted to C 1s form of Nb2CTx, which are assigned to O-CO, C-O, C-C, and C-Nb bonds.

Optical properties of the catalysts are presented in Fig. 5 , where Fig. 5 a illustrates the confocal fluorescence results of the photocatalysts under the same excitation beam, which along with UV-Vis spectroscopy and PL fluorescence results, presented in Fig. S5 demonstrate successful structural transformation from rGO to rGQDs. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S5a -b, the rGQDs exhibited better solvent dispersion than rGO, displaying excitation-dependent fluorescence (Fig. S5c) as shown in the green emission region (around 500 nm). The fluorescence observed in samples containing rGQDs is attributed to inherent fluorescence or strong absorption capability of rGQDs within the UV range (Fig. S5d), confirming successful synthesis of UiO-66@rGQDs and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs. Fig. 5 b presents UV–vis data of Nb2CTx, UiO-66, rGQDs, UiO-66@rGQDs, Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocomposites, where Nb2CTx exhibits metallic characteristics without an absorption edge or bandgap width in UV–Vis DRS spectra. The Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQD nanocomposite presents a more increased absorbance at 200–700 nm than those of UiO-66 and UiO-66@rGQDs (blue curve in Fig. 5 b) and Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs (red curve in Fig. 5 b) spectra.

The transient photocurrent response, which is a valuable measurement in photocatalysts, was also conducted to investigate the photoactivity and charge carrier dynamics. Under the same irradiated visible light source, pure Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, and various ratios of Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs (10:2, 10:3, 10:4, 10:5) as well as 3D-printed nanocomposite samples with and without AuTOHs are compared in Fig. 5 d-e and S7. As expected, Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs showed significantly higher photocurrent intensity compared to pristine Nb2CTx and UiO-66@rGQDs (Fig. 5 d), indicating a better charge separation in the nanocomposites. The combination of UiO-66@rGQDs and Nb2CTx increased the photocurrent, but when the ratio was exceeded to 10:3 (Nb2CTx: UiO-66@rGQDs), the photocurrent decreased (Fig. S7). Therefore, Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs with a ratio of 10:3 was chosen for printing the 3D scaffolds and to perform photocatalytic degradation of simazine. The photocurrent of 3D-printed samples was more stable over the nine on-off light cycles compared to powder sample. Furthermore, the photocurrent intensity of AuTOH-coated 3D composite was significantly higher than that of the uncoated sample (Fig. 5 e). The incorporation of plasmonic Au nanoparticles onto the surface of 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs nanocatalyst has effectively enhanced the light absorption and improved the e-/h+ separation capability.

Simazine degradation in the water was catalyzed by a variety of materials including Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, and AuTOH-3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs evaluated under UV and visible light illuminations as shown in Fig. 6 and Table 1. Simazine solution at C0 exhibits two absorption peaks at 222 and 263 nm, respectively corresponding to electronic transitions π → π* and n → π* [38]. The degradation of simazine under UV irradiation mainly resulted in the formation of by-products appearing in the range of 210–240 nm, thereby affecting the evaluation of degradation efficiency at 222 nm peak (Fig. S8a). Therefore, the intensity at 263 nm peak is considered for the evaluation of simazine degradation efficiency and the rate.

The degradation efficiency of simazine obtained by evaluating the intensity at 263 nm peak of UV-Vis spectrum was compared with the results obtained by GC-MS analysis (Fig. 6 and Fig. S8b -d) that showed similarities. Therefore, UV-Vis spectroscopy with the advantages of being easy to obtain at low cost, simple to operate, being fast, and environmentally friendly was prioritized for further photocatalytic experiments.

The degradation efficiency and kinetic constants, k values were calculated using: [1-(C/C0)]x100. The degradation rate constant (k) followed the pseudo-first-order described as: -ln(C/C0) = kt. The samples reached adsorption-desorption equilibrium after stirring in dark conditions for 40 min (Fig. S9a ). Under the UV irradiation, the % degradation of the catalysts fall in the sequence: Nb2CTx < UiO-66@rGQDs < Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs < 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs < AuTOH-3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs (Fig. 6 a, Table 1). AuTOH-3D-printed Nb2CTx/ UiO-66@rGQDs exhibited the highest decomposition efficiency with a simazine degradation of 90.79 % in 12 min.

Under the effect of visible irradiation wavelength, 3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs and AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs composite catalysts exhibited photodegradation efficiency of 73.01 % after 120 min and 88.31 % after 80 min, respectively. The simazine degradation of 3D-printed sample in the presence of AuTOH (0.02315 min⁻¹) was approximately 1.6-times faster than the sample without AuTOH (0.01510 min⁻¹). The UV and visible light photocatalytic efficiency between 3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs with and without AuTOH incorporation was compared. As shown in Fig. 6 a-d and Table 1, there is a slight difference in photocatalytic efficiency, and the rate between 3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs and AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs under UV irradiation. The visible-light photocatalytic degradation of simazine over 3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs and AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs showed a more distinct difference. The presence of AuTOH had a significant impact on photocatalytic activity, as AuTOH exhibited maximum LSPR efficiency under the visible light, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance, achieving higher degradation efficiency in a shorter time. The photocatalyst performance including simazine degradation efficiency and rate constant of the developed catalyst were compared with the recent publications to highlight the efficiency of our photocatalytic material, as presented in Table 1.

The significant difference in photodegradation rate of simazine between UV (0.1936 min⁻¹) and visible source (0.02315 min⁻¹) is attributed to irradiation intensity of the equipment. Photocatalytic efficiency can vary with changes in the irradiation source and power intensity. In this study, the UV source (600 W) used had a much higher power than the visible source (300 W). The UV light source has higher energy, resulting in a faster and more efficient reaction in breaking the chemical bonds of simazine. These experimental results are consistent with the previous studies comparing the photodegradation performance between the light sources of UV/visible light [39] and LEDs [40]. While the difference in light intensity (UV: 600 W; visible: 300 W) is a significant factor, the difference in degradation rates under UV and visible light can be partially attributed to absorption behavior of the catalyst. Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs and UiO-66@rGQDs exhibit strong absorbance in the UV range. The UiO-66@rGQDs absorb sharply around 306 nm, while Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs show broader absorption extending to longer UV wavelengths, up to 395 nm. Such an extended absorption explains the higher photocatalytic efficiency under the UV light irradiation. AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs inherits strong UV absorption from Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs. However, AuTOH does not significantly affect UV absorption, but enhances the material’s overall light absorption properties through the LSPR effect in the visible range.

Fig. 6 e illustrates the findings of a study on the impact of pH on photocatalytic degradation of 3D materials. Using AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs catalyst, the UV–visible absorption spectrum changes of simazine under different pH values (pH= 3, 6, 9, and 12) were investigated. The 0.1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M NaOH solutions were prepared for this purpose. Before irradiation, the pH of the solution was adjusted, but it was not monitored throughout the procedure. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 e, the results indicate that simazine degradation efficiency increased with the rising pH, while other variables remained constant. The degradation efficiency increased from 75.86% to 98.28% when the pH of simazine solution was increased from 3 to 12. The increased efficiency in alkaline pH range can be explained based on the increased formation of OH radicals with increasing pH. In alkaline conditions, the concentration of hydroxide ions (OH⁻) in the solution is higher. These ions act as precursors for forming hydroxyl radicals (•OH) when the photocatalyst is activated by light. More OH⁻ available promotes the production of •OH, which resulted in more effective degradation. The photodegradation activity of different numbers of AuTOH-3D printed composite scaffolds (1, 2, and 3 pcs) was evaluated and compared in Fig. S9b. The experiments were conducted under the same conditions and the results indicated that photocatalytic performance was elevated with the increasing number of scaffolds from 1 to 3 pcs. The reusability and stability of AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs were further evaluated and the results are presented in Fig. 6 f and S10. Fig. 6 f shows that there was no obvious decrease in the degradation efficiency after 9 consecutive cycles, demonstrating a good recyclability. No significant loss in catalytic performance was observed, which indicates that the composite retains its structural integrity and functional capacity throughout the multiple cycles. Moreover, XRD data shown in Fig. S10a reveal that there was no change in the structure of AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs after the degradation experiments. In addition, AuTOH nanoparticles remain constant in the 3D printed surface even after reaction as shown in Fig. S10b. These results indicate good adhesion of AuTOHs onto the surface of 3D printed samples as well as excellent stability of the photocatalyst.

The degradation of byproducts and intermediates of simazine by AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs were further monitored using GC-MS. Based on the experimental results and reference to previous research data on simazine (m/z = 201, P0), it is proposed to undergo two main degradation pathways: dealkylation (blue shift of the simazine band at 222 nm) and dechlorination (red shift of the 222 nm band), as described in Fig. 7 and S11 [38] . The products were identified as hydroxy-simazine (m/z = 184, P1), 6-hydroxy-2-acetamido-4-(ethylamino)-1,3,5-triazine (m/z = 198, P2), desethylsimazine (m/z = 174, P3), 6-cloro-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine (m/z = 147, P4), 6-hydroxy-2-amino-4-ethyamino-1,3,5-triazine (m/z = 155, P5), ammeline (m/z = 128, P6), and cyanuric acid (m/z = 130, P7). Under both UV and visible light irradiation, the degradation of simazine predominantly resulted in the formation of ammeline and cyanuric acid, which is less toxic than simazine and poses a minimal risk to humans and animals. Ammeline and cyanuric acid then undergo ring cleavage, resulting in the formation of methylguanidine (m/z = 73, P8) and urea (m/z = 60, P9), respectively. The formed byproducts could ultimately be mineralized to CO2, H2O, and inorganic nitrogen (e.g., ammonia or nitrates) due to continuous attack of the generated radicals (•O2 − and •OH).

Previous publications observed that cyanuric acid is the major product in the photocatalytic degradation of simazine [38,41]. In this study, another product, that is urea was formed through hydrolytic cleavage and oxidative reactions during simazine degradation using the developed AuTOH-3D-printed photocatalyst. Most degradation by-products are less toxic than simazine, particularly those resulting from hydroxylation or full mineralization (cyanuric acid and urea). Cyanuric acid and urea are the final degradation products of simazine in photocatalytic processes, indicating near-complete mineralization. Cyanuric acid exhibits low toxicity and poses no acute threat to humans or organisms at typical environmental concentrations. Additionally, it is considered environmentally safe and is commonly used in pool water treatments without significant health impacts. Urea is a simple organic compound containing amides readily decomposes in the environment into CO2 and NH4 + (ammonium radical) via the biological processes. Urea is a much safer degradation product compared to simazine and its toxic intermediates. In natural conditions, urea is quickly assimilated by the microorganisms or plants, reducing the risk of long-term environmental persistence. The GC spectrum of simazine solution as shown in Fig. S12 revealed a significant reduction and near-complete disappearance of the peaks corresponding to toxic pollutants after completion of the degradation process. This demonstrates that the photocatalytic system developed in this research could effectively minimize the presence of harmful byproducts, indicating its potential for low-toxicity pollutant treatment.

The photodegradation mechanism of 3D printed photocatalytic system AuTOH-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs is proposed as presented in Fig. 8. The valence band (VB) and the conduction band (CB) potentials of UiO-66@rGQDs were identified using UV–Vis DRS spectra and Mott-Schottky examination, as illustrated in Fig. 8 a-b. The flat band potentials (Vfb, V vs. Ag/AgCl) and band gap of UiO-66@rGQDs were determined at −0.20 V and +4.05 eV, respectively. The electronic band structures of UiO-66@rGQDs were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2) [25], in which EVB and ECB vs NHE (ECB/EVB) for UiO-66@rGQDs are −0.10/+ 3.95 eV.

When AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs catalysts system are excited by the light, the outer AuTOH layer receives the photon energy first and photogenerates a series of electrons (e-), which then continuously can flow into 3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs scaffolds via the permeation mechanism [42]. AuTOHs are the conductive agents and strong resonators, enhancing the visible light absorption capacity through surface plasmon resonance, which is consistent with higher photocurrent intensity of AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs. The ECB of UiO-66@rGQDs is slightly higher than the Fermi level of Nb2CTx and AuTOHs; therefore, there are two transformation and migration mechanisms for the photogenerated electrons (Fig. 8 c). The electrons on the surface of UiO-66@rGQDs are transferred to Nb2CTx; the photogenerated electrons onto the surface of AuTOHs can also be transferred to Nb2CTx due to LSPR effect, which then react with the adsorbed O2 to form •O2 −. Nb2CTx with excellent conductivity traps of the electrons, thus reducing recombination and enhancing the charge separation efficiency of the photocatalytic system. In addition, the electrons on the surface of AuTOHs can also react with the adsorbed O2 to yield superoxide radical anions (O2 −). The EVB of UiO-66@rGQDs is higher than the standard potential of H2O/•OH, thus easily oxidizing the holes directly to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These radicals •O2 − and (•OH) continue to attack simazine, generating intermediates and thus, decomposing the simazine. The attachment of AuTOH particles onto 3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs scaffolds and the provision of more adsorption and active sites from the incorporation of Nb2CTx into the composites improve the photocatalytic efficiency. The photocatalytic mechanism of AuTOH-Nb₂CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs system can be described through the following chemical reactions, emphasizing the electron transfer and reactive species formation.
(3) UiO-66@rGQDs + ℎν → UiO-66@rGQDs (e⁻ + h⁺)
(4) AuTOH + ℎν (LSPR) → AuTOH (hot e⁻)
(5) UiO-66@rGQDs (e⁻) → Nb₂CTₓ
(6) AuTOH (hot e⁻) → Nb₂CTₓ
(7) O₂ + e− → ·O₂⁻
(8) H₂O + ℎ+ → ·OH + H⁺
(9) Simazine + ROS → Degradation products

The ESR experiments were further used to confirm the main active substances responsible for the photodegradation of simazine using AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs photocatalyst. DMPO was used as the spin trapping agent for superoxide radicals (•O2 -) and hydroxyl •OH radicals, which was prepared in water for the capture of •OH radical and prepared in methanol for the capture of •O2 -. As the results shown in Fig. 8 d, six typical peaks of DMPO-•O2 - and four characteristic peaks of DMPO-•OH were detected under the light irradiation. In addition, the intensity of DMPO-•O2 - radical signal was stronger than that of DMPO-•OH, indicating that •O2 - radicals played the dominant role in photocatalytic degradation of simazine.

Reactive species in the photocatalyst for simazine degradation using AuTOH-3D printed-Nb2CTₓ/UiO-66@rGQDs were investigated through quenching experiment, as presented in Fig. S13a. Numerous scavengers including isopropyl alcohol (IPA, •OH quenching), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, h+ quenching), and ascorbic acid (AA, •O2 − quenching) were added to simazine solution (pollutant) before the introduction of 3D scaffold. The aim of this experiment was to confirm the key radicals during the degradation of simazine and to elucidate the mechanism underlying the photocatalytic degradation. The significance of the corresponding reactive species was indicated by the extent of simazine degradation brought about by these scavengers during the conversion process. The results showed that simazine degradation efficiency of the catalyst was considerably decreased by the addition of EDTA, AA, and IPA, the values decreasing from 90.79% to 84.17%, 54.12%, and 77.67%, respectively. This suggests that the reactive oxygen species engaged in photocatalytic activities were influenced by ·OH, ·O2 −, and h⁺ radicals. This observation implies that the main reactive species essential to the photocatalytic breakdown of simazine are •O2 − (superoxide radicals). These findings align with the ESR results shown in Fig. 8 d. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curve was determined to investigate the dynamics of photogenerated charge carriers and to understand the mechanism of 3D printed photocatalytic system AuTOH-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs (Fig. S13b). The PL lifetime of e-/h+ pair recombination was 3.5 ns for AuTOH-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs photocatalyst, indicating the efficient charge separation for enhancing photocatalytic performance.

In this study, a novel persistent catalyst system based on 3D-printed Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs integrated with AuTOHs was prepared to investigate the photodecomposition of simazine, a persistent herbicide having potentially toxic effects on humans and the ecosystem. Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs were synthesized with the ratio of Nb2CTx to UiO-66@rGQDs. FE-SEM, XRD, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, Raman, and confocal fluorescence analyses demonstrated successful synthesis of the nanocomposites. The octahedral UiO-66@rGQDs particles dispersed into the pores and onto the surface of Nb2CTx formed a strong interfacial interaction between them. Following the self-assembly method, AuTOH NPs (115 nm diameter) were successfully attached to 3D printed scaffold-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs, exhibiting outstanding photocatalytic activity. The particles exhibited a rate constant of 0.1936 min−1 within 12 min under UV irradiation and 0.02315 min⁻¹ within 80 min under the visible light, AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs catalyst showed significantly increased activity compared to Nb2CTx, UiO-66@rGQDs, and 3D printed-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs. The generation of ROS including •OH, •O2 −, and h+ was confirmed by ESR analysis and chemical scavenging experiments, finding that superoxide radicals play essential roles in the simazine degradation by the AuTOH-3D-Nb2CTx/UiO-66@rGQDs system. The combination of 3D printing technology with photocatalysts has shown the potential of efficient and easy recycling after 9 cycles. In addition, the mechanism and by-products of this photocatalytic process are proposed. Through a comprehensive investigation, this study sought to contribute to developing sustainable solutions for simazine remediation, addressing both environmental concerns and public health-related issues.
